title: Managing a broody hen
subtitle: Breaking broodiness, or using it to hatch a clutch.
excerpt: A broody hen stops laying and can sit unproductively for weeks if not managed. This covers how to recognise broodiness, the options for breaking it, and how to use a broody hen successfully to hatch a clutch of fertile eggs if that is what you want.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Laying Hens (2018). Practical Poultry Magazine, broody management features. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), poultry chapters.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hormonally driven behaviour in hens where the bird becomes determined to sit on a nest and incubate eggs. A broody hen stops laying, flattens over the nest when approached, and will sit even on an empty nest or infertile eggs.
A set of eggs a broody hen sits on to incubate. In a managed setting, the clutch is placed under the broody hen on the first day of sitting; incubation takes 21 days for most domestic chicken breeds.

content.text: by sitting tight in the nest box, fluffing up and growling when disturbed, and returning to the nest immediately after being moved off. She will have stopped laying. Left unmanaged, a broody hen can sit for three weeks or more, losing body condition throughout. You either break the broodiness or you use it.

Remove the hen from the nest box and block access for the rest of the day; repeat each morning. If the hen returns to broodiness, move her to a wire-floored cage raised off the ground with food and water but no nesting material. The airflow beneath the cage reduces the body temperature that sustains the broody drive. Three to five days in the cage breaks most hens. Return her to the flock when she is eating normally, moving around, and no longer seeking a nest.
Using a broody to hatch eggs

Move the broody to a separate, quiet space away from other hens: shared nest boxes lead to other hens laying into the clutch, which disrupts the hatch date and can result in the broody abandoning early-stage eggs when late-stage eggs start hatching. Place the fertile eggs under her at night when she is settled. A standard-sized hen covers eight to twelve eggs comfortably; too many and outer eggs chill.

Check daily that the hen is leaving the nest once or twice to eat, drink, and defecate. A hen that does not leave the nest for more than 48 hours is at risk of dehydration and weight loss; lift her off the nest gently and place her at the food and water. Mark the

eggs with a pencil mark so any additional eggs laid into the nest by other hens can be identified and removed. Incubation takes 21 days; candle at day 7 to check fertility and remove clear (infertile) eggs.
